findings.title: No LLM calls, no I/O, no user input — no injection surface
findings.description: platt_scaling.py is pure math. No LLM calls, no file I/O, no network access, no user-facing endpoints. The module only imports math, dataclasses, and datetime. There is no injection vector.

findings.title: Division safety: zero-checks in place
findings.description: Newton's method: det == 0 check on line 65 with early break prevents division by zero in the Hessian inverse computation. ECE: b['count'] == 0 check on line 121 skips empty buckets, preventing division by zero in accuracy/average calculation. Both zero-guards are correctly placed.

findings.title: No log(0) risk — math.exp() has no domain restrictions
findings.description: The sigmoid implementation uses math.exp() which accepts any real number (including negative infinity → 0.0). No log() calls exist in the module. Numerically stable sigmoid: positive x uses 1/(1+exp(-x)), negative x uses exp(x)/(1+exp(x)), both avoiding overflow.

findings.title: Edge case: no input validation on confidence/outcome ranges
findings.description: fit_platt_scaling() does not validate that confidences are in [0,1] or outcomes are 0/1. Negative confidences map to bucket 0 in ECE (safe). Confidences > 1 map to bucket 9 (safe). Non-binary outcomes produce degraded ECE but cannot crash or inject. This is a data quality issue, not a security issue.
findings.remediation: LOW priority. Consider adding input validation: assert all(0 <= c <= 1 for c in confidences) and assert all(o in (0, 1) for o in outcomes). This is defensive coding, not security-critical.

findings.title: NaN propagation is safe
findings.description: If NaN inputs are fed to fit_platt_scaling(), math.exp(NaN) → NaN, errors = outcome - NaN → NaN, gradients become NaN, Newton steps become NaN. The convergence check (abs(grad) < 1e-6) will fail (NaN < 1e-6 is False), so the loop runs max_iter times and returns NaN coefficients. Downstream apply_platt_scaling returns NaN. This produces garbage calibration but no crash, no injection, no data corruption beyond the NaN value itself.
findings.remediation: LOW priority. Could add isnan checks at function entry, but the current behavior is safe (no crash, no injection).

notes: Pure math module with no LLM calls, no I/O, no user input. All division operations have zero-guards. Numerically stable sigmoid implementation. Low-risk tier — PICA-Security requirements are satisfied. Input validation on confidence/outcome ranges would be a nice-to-have but is not security-critical.
